proposal.reasoning: This is the first iteration in the exploration phase with no prior data. I am proposing 4 diverse parameter sets to sample different regions of parameter space: (1) Baseline - central values to establish a reference point; (2) High Cohesion - increased epsilon magnitudes (more negative) and reduced sigma to test if stronger attractions improve density and heat of vaporization; (3) High Dipole - maximized dummy bead bond lengths and charges to test the upper limit of dipole moment effects on electronic properties and surface tension; (4) Low Cohesion/High Size - reduced epsilon magnitudes and increased sigma to test sensitivity to weaker intermolecular forces. This covers the full range of physically plausible behaviors while respecting constraints (dummy bonds < sigma/2, equal/opposite charges, force constants > 100).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing epsilon parameters from weak (-0.9, -0.5, -0.7) to strong (-1.15, -0.75, -0.95) will modulate cohesive energy density, directly affecting density, heat of vaporization, and surface tension because stronger Lennard-Jones attractions lead to tighter packing and higher energy required for vaporization. Simultaneously, varying dummy bead bond lengths (1.0 to 1.9 Å) and charges (±0.35 to ±0.5) controls the dipole moment magnitude (μ = q × d), which affects dielectric properties and surface polarization.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Fork 2 (high cohesion) should increase density and heat of vaporization toward target values (0.9361 g/cm³, 10.951 kcal/mol) by strengthening intermolecular attractions. hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 4 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations, comparing calculated density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, and dipole moment against experimental targets to determine which direction (stronger/weaker cohesion, larger/smaller dipole) improves agreement.
